author: unknown
body: Multiple developers capturing, amending, and deprecating decisions must be able to share and merge their work without silent data loss. The current import is additive-only and skips existing records, which drops status changes and metadata updates.
title: Dictum must support team workflows with conflict resolution for concurrent decision-making

author: tomas
body: When scope enumeration is needed, add read-only commands (dictum scopes, dictum labels) rather than a vocabulary registry. Scopes stay as free-text. Upgrade to first-class entities only if drift becomes a real problem.

links.reason: discoverability is the lightweight path to vocabulary consistency

rebuttal: if free-text scopes cause real vocabulary drift problems
title: Scope discoverability commands before scope-as-entity

author: tomas
body: If a command would read from stdin and stdin is a terminal, error immediately with usage guidance instead of silently blocking.
title: CLI commands must not hang on TTY stdin

author: tomas
body: open() calls initialize() which uses CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS. A DB file that exists but has missing tables recovers automatically instead of erroring.

links.reason: self-heal is how we cope with schema instability

title: DB connections self-heal missing tables

author: tomas
body: Test enum round-trips (Display/FromStr agreement), filter correctness (SQL building), and DB field persistence. Skip CLI arg parsing, text formatting, and basic CRUD.

links.reason: testing strategy for the dogfooded tool

title: Test coverage targets important workflows, not exhaustive

author: tomas
body: Project is early stage. No migration tests or backwards-compat tests needed yet. Schema can change freely.

links.reason: schema freedom enables rapid model iteration
self-heal is how we cope with schema instability

rebuttal: when dictum has real users or production data
title: OK to break old databases and JSONL formats

author: tomas
body: Scopes, labels, and terminology are free-text strings. The tool is a dumb store with good queryability. The LLM agent reads existing vocabulary and enforces consistency via the skill file. The human is the authority on preferred terms.

links.reason: vocabulary consistency is part of how typed propositions work in practice
discoverability is the lightweight path to vocabulary consistency

title: Vocabulary consistency is enforced by the LLM, not the data model

author: tomas
body: Each decision has a kind (principle, constraint, assumption, choice, rule, goal) and weight (must, should, may). Machines reason about each differently: axioms are never questioned, assumptions are monitored, rules are enforced unconditionally.

links.reason: typed propositions serve the dogfooding goal
vocabulary consistency is part of how typed propositions work in practice
schema freedom enables rapid model iteration

title: Decisions are typed propositions with machine-distinct semantics

author: tomas
body: Dictum tracks its own decisions. This validates the tool and keeps project direction explicit.

links.reason: typed propositions serve the dogfooding goal
testing strategy for the dogfooded tool

title: Dogfood dictum on its own development

author: tomas
title: Tool must provide guardrails to AI agents to steer development in accordance with existing decisions
